Guideline: Read signals inside a tracking scope
Description: Signals only notify subscribers when called within a tracking scope such as JSX, createEffect, or createMemo.
Do: Access the signal getter inside JSX or an effect so updates propagate.
Don't: Read a signal outside any tracking scope and expect it to re-run on change.
Code Bad: console.log(count()); setCount(1); // not tracked, never re-runs

Guideline: Call signals as functions, never store the value
Description: createSignal returns a getter function; you must call it to read the current, reactive value.
Do: Invoke the getter, e.g. count(), everywhere you need the live value.
Don't: Read once into a plain variable and reuse it as if reactive.

Guideline: Keep reactive reads out of the component body top level
Description: Components run only once on init, so signals read directly in the body run once and never update.
Do: Put signal reads in the JSX return or inside createEffect.
Don't: Read a signal at the top of the component and rely on it updating.
Code Bad: console.log(count()); // runs once at init only

Guideline: Create state with createSignal
Description: createSignal takes an initial value and returns a [getter, setter] pair via array destructuring.
Do: Destructure the returned pair into a getter and setter.
Don't: Treat the return value as a plain value instead of a getter/setter pair.
Code Bad: const count = createSignal(0); // count is the pair, not the value

Guideline: Use the functional setter for derived updates
Description: The setter accepts a function receiving the previous value, which is safest when the new value depends on the old.
Do: Pass prev => prev + 1 when updating based on current state.
Don't: Hardcode a value that ignores the latest state.

Guideline: Reserve createEffect for side effects, not derived state
Description: Effects are for actions outside the reactive system like DOM manipulation, data fetching, and subscriptions.
Do: Use effects for I/O and external synchronization.
Don't: Compute a derived value inside an effect instead of a memo.

Guideline: Avoid setting signals inside effects
Description: Writing signals from an effect can cause extra renders or infinite loops; compute values with createMemo instead.
Do: Derive values with createMemo and keep effects side-effect only.
Don't: Set a signal in an effect that feeds back into its own dependencies.

Guideline: Let Solid auto-track dependencies
Description: Solid automatically tracks every signal read inside an effect, so manual dependency lists are unnecessary.
Do: Read the signals you need directly inside the callback.
Don't: Try to declare or pass an explicit dependency array.
Code Bad: createEffect(() => log(count()), [count]); // no dep arrays in Solid

Guideline: Use onMount for once-only side effects
Description: onMount runs a callback a single time after mount without tracking, ideal for one-time API calls.
Do: Wrap one-time fetches in onMount.
Don't: Rely on createEffect to run only once.

Guideline: Clean up subscriptions with onCleanup
Description: onCleanup runs on unmount to release timers and subscriptions and prevent memory leaks.
Do: Clear intervals, listeners, and subscriptions in onCleanup.
Don't: Leave a setInterval or subscription running after unmount.

Guideline: Prefer createMemo for expensive or reused derivations
Description: A derived signal re-runs on every access, while a memo caches and recomputes only when dependencies change.
Do: Wrap expensive or multiply-used computations in createMemo.
Don't: Reuse a plain derived function many times for costly work.
Code Bad: const double = () => expensive(count()); // recomputed each call

Guideline: Keep memos pure
Description: A memo's function should be side-effect free; setting signals inside can cause infinite loops.
Do: Return a computed value and nothing else from a memo.
Don't: Set a signal or trigger side effects inside a memo.

Guideline: Use a plain derived signal for cheap values
Description: Simple, inexpensive derivations can just be a function that reads signals, no memo needed.
Do: Define const double = () => count() * 2 for trivial derivations.
Don't: Reach for createMemo on trivial one-off computations by reflex.
Code Bad: const double = createMemo(() => count() * 2); // overkill if used once

Guideline: Follow one-way data flow: state, view, action
Description: State is the source of truth, the view renders it, and actions update it, keeping updates predictable.
Do: Update state only through setters triggered by actions.
Don't: Mutate rendered values directly to change the UI.

Guideline: Lift shared state to a common ancestor
Description: When multiple components need the same state, hoist it to a shared parent and pass values and setters down.
Do: Hold state in the ancestor and pass setCount to children.
Don't: Duplicate the same signal in sibling components.
Code Bad: // each child owns its own createSignal copy

Guideline: Never destructure props
Description: Destructuring reads the value once and breaks reactivity; access props.x or wrap in a function.
Do: Read props.name directly or use () => props.name.
Don't: Destructure props into local constants.

Guideline: Treat props as read-only
Description: Props flow one way and cannot be mutated by the child; pass a setter to change parent state.
Do: Call a setter passed from the parent to update state.
Don't: Assign to props to change parent data.

Guideline: Set default prop values with mergeProps
Description: mergeProps merges reactive objects like Object.assign while preserving reactivity of each property.
Do: Use mergeProps to layer defaults under incoming props.
Don't: Spread props into a plain object, losing reactivity.

Guideline: Split props with splitProps, not destructuring
Description: splitProps separates a props object into groups while keeping each property reactive.
Do: Use splitProps to forward subsets of props reactively.
Don't: Destructure to split props before forwarding them.

Guideline: Wrap repeated children access with the children helper
Description: Accessing props.children multiple times can recreate child elements; the children helper memoizes them.
Do: Resolve children once via children(() => props.children).
Don't: Reference props.children repeatedly in the same render.

Guideline: Use Context to avoid prop drilling
Description: When many nested components need the same data, Context supplies it without threading props through each layer.
Do: Provide shared data through Context for deep trees.
Don't: Pass the same prop through many intermediate components.

Guideline: Use <Show> for conditional rendering
Description: <Show> renders children when its when prop is truthy and supports a fallback, cleaner than inline ternaries.
Do: Gate JSX with <Show when={...} fallback={...}>.
Don't: Nest raw ternaries in JSX for show/hide logic.

Guideline: Use <Switch>/<Match> for exclusive conditions
Description: <Switch> evaluates <Match> children in order and renders the first truthy one, like switch/case.
Do: Group mutually exclusive branches in <Switch> with a fallback.
Don't: Chain deeply nested <Show> components for many conditions.

Guideline: Render arrays with <For>, not Array.map
Description: <For> is the control-flow component for dynamic lists and preserves fine-grained reactivity when items reorder.
Do: Loop collections with <For each={items()}>.
Don't: Use items().map() inside JSX for reactive lists.

Guideline: Use <For> for arrays of objects where order changes
Description: <For> keys rows by reference and moves DOM nodes on reorder; its index is a signal called as index().
Do: Use <For> for object lists that reorder or resize.
Don't: Use <Index> when items are objects that shift position.

Guideline: Use <Index> for primitives and stable inputs
Description: When list length/order is stable but content changes (e.g. input fields), <Index> re-renders less; item is a signal.
Do: Use <Index> for arrays of strings/numbers and form inputs.
Don't: Use <For> for primitive lists that only change values.

Guideline: Use createStore for nested, complex state
Description: Stores use proxies for fine-grained reactivity, updating only changed properties instead of triggering full re-renders.
Do: Manage deeply nested objects/arrays with createStore.
Don't: Hold large nested objects in a single signal.

Guideline: Read store values inside a tracking scope
Description: Store signals are created lazily on first access within a tracking scope, so reads outside are not tracked.
Do: Access store.x inside JSX or createEffect to establish dependencies.
Don't: Read store properties at the top level and expect updates.

Guideline: Update stores with path syntax setters
Description: Path syntax targets the exact key/index to change, triggering reactivity only for affected paths.
Do: Use setStore('users', i, 'loggedIn', false) style targeted updates.
Don't: Replace the whole array with a spread when only one item changes.

Guideline: Batch multiple mutations with produce
Description: produce gives a mutable draft so several properties update in one call instead of many setter calls.
Do: Use produce for grouped mutations on objects/arrays.
Don't: Issue many separate setStore calls for one logical change.

Guideline: Merge fetched data with reconcile
Description: reconcile diffs incoming data against the store and updates only changed values, avoiding needless updates.
Do: Wrap new server data in reconcile when setting a store.
Don't: Overwrite the store wholesale on every refetch.

Guideline: Use unwrap for non-reactive raw data
Description: unwrap returns a plain object snapshot for use outside tracking scopes or with third-party libraries.
Do: Call unwrap(store) before handing data to external code.
Don't: Pass the reactive proxy directly to libraries expecting plain objects.

Guideline: Navigate with the <A> component
Description: The <A> component adds routing features like activeClass and inactiveClass over a plain anchor tag.
Do: Use <A href='/users'> for in-app navigation.
Don't: Use raw anchors when you need active-state styling.

Guideline: Code-split routes with lazy
Description: lazy postpones loading a route component until it is navigated to, reducing initial bundle size.
Do: Wrap route components in lazy(() => import(...)).
Don't: Import every page eagerly at the app entry.

Guideline: Read dynamic params with useParams
Description: useParams exposes reactive route parameters defined with the :param syntax.
Do: Access params.id via const params = useParams().
Don't: Parse the URL manually to get route parameters.

Guideline: Preload route data in parallel
Description: A preload function starts data fetching alongside route loading, or eagerly on link hover.
Do: Attach preload={fn} to a <Route> to fetch data early.
Don't: Start all fetching only after the component mounts.
Code Bad: // fetch inside the component after navigation completes

Guideline: Validate params with matchFilters
Description: matchFilters constrains route params by enum, regex, or predicate so non-matching URLs do not match the route.
Do: Pass matchFilters to enforce param shape (e.g. numeric id).
Don't: Accept any param string and validate manually in the component.
Code Bad: <Route path='/users/:id' /> // then check typeof id in body
